headline: The relief holds: oil slides to ~$71 as the Hormuz war turns to talks, India rallies a second day (Sensex +827), Nvidia breaks out +4% — but foreigners keep quietly selling India.
market_story: Markets kept doing the opposite of the scary headlines, because the headlines got less scary. The US-Iran fight over the Strait of Hormuz — the sea gate for a fifth of the world's oil — turned from strikes to negotiation: Washington says Iran privately called this week's tanker attack 'a mistake,' Iran's foreign minister flew to Oman to arrange safe passage, and the fighting 'appears to have paused.' So the 'war premium' kept draining out of crude — US oil (WTI) slid to about $71 a barrel, its RSI (a momentum gauge) down near 38, deep in 'oversold' territory — and that single fact rippled outward. Wall Street's fear gauge (the VIX) fell another ~5% to ~15, US indices edged to fresh highs, and Nvidia broke out +4% to $211, reclaiming its 50-day average after weeks stuck below it. India booked a second straight relief rally: the Sensex rose 827 points (+1.08%) to 77,569 and the Nifty +1.02% to 24,207, with banks and mid/small-caps leading and the rupee firming toward 95.3 as oil eased. But look under the hood and there's a catch that matters for a beginner: a report showed foreign investors have pulled ~60% of the money they'd put into India-focused funds since 2024 (about $9bn in 2026 alone), chasing the AI boom and US stocks — so this rally is being powered by domestic savers (record monthly SIPs), not foreigners. The takeaway: when a market has already priced fear, 'less bad' is enough to lift it — but a rally leaning on one engine (home money) while another (foreign money) leaves is worth watching.

board.name: S&P 500
board.read: US large-caps ticked to a fresh high as the war premium faded — a calm, grinding risk-on, not a melt-up.

board.name: Nasdaq
board.read: Tech held its gains; the AI-chip complex steadied after SK Hynix's blockbuster $26.5bn debut.

board.name: Dow Jones
board.read: Blue chips edged up in line with the broad market — a steady, low-drama session.

board.name: VIX (fear gauge)
board.read: The fear gauge fell to ~15 — investors expect little turbulence now; calm, but also complacency.

board.name: Nifty 50
board.read: India's benchmark rose a second day, holding above 24,000 as oil eased and risk appetite returned.

board.name: Sensex
board.read: Up 827 points, extending the rebound; broad-based with banks and smaller stocks leading.

board.name: Bank Nifty
board.read: Banks outpaced the index — calmer oil and a firmer rupee ease the inflation-and-rate worry that pressures lenders.

board.name: Bitcoin
board.read: Barely moved and still below its 50- and 200-day averages — a bounce that hasn't yet turned the downtrend.

board.name: Ethereum
board.read: Firmed slightly with the risk-on tone; sits between its key averages (transitional), like the broad crypto tape.

board.name: Gold
board.read: Slipped again — with panic fading and the dollar firm, the safe-haven bid keeps softening.

board.name: Crude Oil (WTI)
board.read: The key number: oil fell to ~$71 as Hormuz turned diplomatic — RSI ~38 (oversold), the war premium is gone.

board.name: USD/INR
board.read: The rupee firmed (fewer rupees per dollar) as oil eased — direct relief for import-heavy India.

themes.note: SK Hynix soared ~13% on its record $26.5bn Nasdaq debut and Nvidia broke out +4% above its 50-day average — the AI-hardware trade steadied even amid 'AI fatigue' chatter.
Bank Nifty (+1.4%) led a second-day rally; calmer oil and a firmer rupee ease the rate-and-inflation drag on lenders, and mid/small-caps outperformed.
WTI slid to ~$71 (RSI oversold) as Hormuz de-escalated — relief for oil-consumers (airlines, paints, logistics); Reliance bounced with the tape but stays technically weak.
Gold eased and the VIX fell to ~15 as the panic unwound — the classic mirror image of a fear day.

what_to_watch.title: Hormuz turns to diplomacy — oil slides to ~$71
key_points: US says Iran privately called its tanker attack 'a mistake'
Iran's FM in Oman to arrange safe passage; fighting 'paused'
WTI ~$71 (-0.9%), RSI ~38 — the war premium has fully deflated
But Trump still threatens missiles; a naval 'blockade' stays
what_to_watch.gist: The oil scare that started the week has all but reversed. Instead of Iran closing the Strait of Hormuz, US officials say Tehran privately called its ship attack 'a mistake,' its foreign minister has flown to Oman to guarantee safe passage, and the shooting has paused. US crude fell to about $71 a barrel — its RSI, a momentum gauge that runs 0–100, sits near 38, meaning it's technically 'oversold' (sold off hard, often due a bounce).
what_to_watch.why_it_matters: Oil is the master variable for this market: it drives inflation, the rupee and whether India's rally can hold. A 'war premium' (extra price paid to insure against a supply cut) inflates on fear and deflates when the feared cutoff doesn't arrive. It has now fully deflated — which is exactly why stocks could keep rising and the rupee could firm.
what_to_watch.what_it_means: For an oil-importer like India, ~$71 crude versus ~$85 is the difference between a relief rally and a fresh sell-off — so this one number keeps setting the tone for the rupee, inflation and Indian equities. The risk is asymmetric: any fresh Hormuz incident would snap the premium straight back.
what_to_watch.watch: Whether Iran makes the public 'strait is open' pledge Washington wants, and whether WTI holds ~$71 or bounces from oversold.

key_terms.definition: Extra money buyers pay for oil to insure against conflict cutting supply — it can fall even during a standoff if barrels keep flowing.
The Relative Strength Index measures momentum from 0–100; below ~30 is 'oversold' (sold off hard, often due a bounce), above ~70 'overbought'.

what_to_watch.the_lesson: What moves oil is lost barrels, not scary headlines — once a standoff turns to negotiation, the price premium drains fast, so watch the diplomacy and the supply flows, not just the conflict.

what_to_watch.title: India rallies again — but foreigners are the sellers
key_points: Second up day; banks and mid/small-caps lead
Foreigners pulled ~60% of India-fund inflows since 2024 (~$9bn in 2026)
Domestic SIPs (record monthly flows) are powering the rally
what_to_watch.gist: India's market rose a second straight day — the Sensex up 827 points to 77,569 and the Nifty back above 24,000 — as cooling oil and a firmer rupee lifted sentiment, with banks and smaller stocks leading. But a report showed foreign investors have withdrawn nearly 60% of the money they'd put into India-focused funds since a 2024 peak (about $9 billion in 2026 alone), chasing AI and US stocks instead.
what_to_watch.why_it_matters: It means the rally has two engines running opposite ways: foreigners selling, domestic savers buying. Indian households now invest a steady, record sum every month through SIPs (systematic investment plans — fixed monthly mutual-fund contributions), and that home-grown bid is now strong enough to lift the index alone. That makes India less hostage to global mood swings — but more reliant on domestic confidence holding.
what_to_watch.what_it_means: For anyone holding Indian stocks, the takeaway is that the market's floor is increasingly domestic — resilient, but watch the monthly SIP number and whether foreign selling slows. If the AI trade wobbles, some of that foreign money could even rotate back to cheaper India.
what_to_watch.watch: Whether the Nifty holds above 24,000, the pace of monthly SIP inflows, and any slowdown in FII selling.

key_terms.definition: Money foreign institutional investors move into/out of a country's stocks; heavy selling can pull a market and its currency down.
A systematic investment plan — a fixed sum an Indian saver invests in mutual funds every month, creating a steady, home-grown bid for stocks.

what_to_watch.the_lesson: A market can rise even while foreigners sell — when a country's own savers invest steadily and at scale, they can become the dominant force, but the rally then rests on domestic confidence, not global flows.

what_to_watch.title: Nvidia breaks out +4% as SK Hynix's record debut soars
key_points: Nvidia +4% to $211, reclaiming its 50-day average (~$209)
Flips from 'stuck sideways' to a confirmed uptrend (Strong Buy)
SK Hynix jumps ~13% on its record $26.5bn Nasdaq debut
Yet Jefferies flags investors talking of 'AI fatigue'
what_to_watch.gist: The AI-chip trade came back to life. SK Hynix, a Korean memory-chip maker, soared ~13% on its Nasdaq debut after raising a record $26.5 billion — a real-money vote that AI-hardware demand still has legs. Nvidia jumped about 4% to $211 and, crucially, closed back above its 50-day average (~$209) after weeks stuck below it — flipping its trend from 'sideways/neutral' to a confirmed uptrend.
what_to_watch.why_it_matters: A moving average is the average price over a set window (here 50 days); price reclaiming it is a classic sign a stalled uptrend is resuming. For Nvidia, which had been range-bound and rated Neutral just yesterday, that's a genuine technical turn. But the picture isn't one-sided: strategists at Jefferies flagged growing 'AI fatigue' — waning enthusiasm for AI stocks after a long run — even as they stayed bullish on memory chips long term.
what_to_watch.what_it_means: For holders of Nvidia or the AI complex, the breakout is encouraging, but 'AI fatigue' talk means the easy gains may be maturing — expect sharper swings around each earnings print, and watch whether Nvidia holds $209.
what_to_watch.watch: Whether Nvidia stays above its 50-day (~$209); first resistance ~$214, first support ~$205. How SK Hynix holds its debut pop.

key_terms.definition: The average price over a set number of days (e.g. 50 or 200); price reclaiming it signals a stalled uptrend may be resuming.
Investor slang for waning enthusiasm for AI-linked stocks after a long run-up — doubt about how much higher the shares can go, not a fall in AI usage.

what_to_watch.the_lesson: A stock reclaiming its 50-day average is a real signal a stalled trend is turning up — but in a crowded theme, momentum and 'fatigue' can flip fast, so confirm the breakout holds rather than chasing one green day.

what_to_watch.title: Bitcoin stalls near $64k — the downtrend is still intact
key_points: Held near $64k despite spot-ETF outflows
Still below its 50-day (~$65,146) and 200-day (~$73,988) averages
Sits near the bottom of its 52-week range (11th percentile)
what_to_watch.gist: Bitcoin barely budged, holding near $64,000 even as money kept flowing out of spot Bitcoin ETFs (funds that hold real Bitcoin and trade like a stock). Zoom out and it's still weak: BTC trades below both its 50-day (~$65,146) and 200-day (~$73,988) averages — a textbook downtrend — and sits near the bottom of its one-year range.
what_to_watch.why_it_matters: Bitcoin has been trading like a high-risk tech bet, not 'digital gold' — it fell on fear days and bounced on risk-on days, the same rhythm as AI stocks. Steady ETF outflows keep a lid on rebounds. A bounce inside a downtrend is common; the trend only turns when price reclaims those falling averages.
what_to_watch.what_it_means: For a newcomer, this is a live lesson that flat is not a bottom and a bounce is not a trend change — Bitcoin needs to close back above ~$65,146 (its 50-day) to signal the downtrend is genuinely turning.
what_to_watch.watch: Whether BTC reclaims its 50-day (~$65,146); first support to hold is ~$64,099.

key_terms.definition: A fund that holds actual Bitcoin and trades like a stock; steady outflows mean investors are pulling money out, which can weigh on the price.
A pattern of falling prices where rebounds tend to fade; trading below key moving averages confirms it.

what_to_watch.the_lesson: A flat day near the lows is not a bottom, and a bounce is not a trend change — judge direction by whether an asset reclaims its key moving averages, not by a single quiet or green session.

what_to_watch.title: India's inflation likely topped the RBI's 4% — the war's quiet bill
key_points: June CPI seen ~4.2% (Mint poll) — first breach of the 4% target
ADB cut India's FY27 growth forecast to 6.6% from 6.9%
HDFC Bank chief flags El Niño / below-normal monsoon risk
Leaves the RBI with less room to cut rates
what_to_watch.gist: India's retail inflation for June likely rose to about 4.2%, above the Reserve Bank of India's 4% medium-term target for the first time under the revised price index, as costlier fuel and food fed through. The ADB has trimmed India's FY27 growth forecast to 6.6% (from 6.9%), and HDFC Bank's MD warned that El Niño and a weak monsoon could add fresh price and growth risk.
what_to_watch.why_it_matters: Inflation is the leash on the central bank: the higher it runs, the less room the RBI has to cut interest rates to support growth. A 4%+ print, a growth downgrade and a monsoon warning are the 'quiet bill' of the oil scare — the macro cost that shows up in the data even on days the market rallies.
what_to_watch.what_it_means: For rate-sensitive sectors (banks, real estate, autos) and for borrowers, sticky inflation means rate relief stays on hold longer — a headwind that outlasts a two-day bounce. If oil holds near $71, though, much of the pressure eases.
what_to_watch.watch: The official June CPI release, the RBI's tone at its next meeting, and monsoon distribution through July–August.

key_terms.definition: The Consumer Price Index tracks how fast the prices households pay are rising; food and fuel are its biggest movers in India.
A periodic warming of the Pacific that can weaken India's monsoon, hurting crops and lifting food prices.

what_to_watch.the_lesson: A market can rebound in a day, but an energy shock's real cost shows up slowly in inflation and growth data — which is ultimately what constrains the central bank and rate-sensitive stocks.

global_summary.us: US indices edged to fresh highs (S&P +0.4%, Nasdaq +0.3%, Dow +0.3%) as the war premium kept fading and the VIX fell ~5% to ~15. Nvidia broke out +4% and SK Hynix's record $26.5bn Nasdaq debut soared ~13%, reviving the AI-chip trade.
global_summary.india: India rallied a second day: the Sensex rose 827 points (+1.08%) to 77,569 and the Nifty +1.02% to 24,207, banks and mid/small-caps leading, the rupee firming toward 95.3. But foreigners have pulled ~60% of their India-fund inflows since 2024 (~$9bn in 2026), leaving domestic SIPs to power the move.
global_summary.crypto: Bitcoin held near $64,400 (+0.4%) and Ether firmed to ~$1,811 despite ETF outflows, but both remain below key averages in a longer downtrend.
global_summary.macro: WTI crude slid to ~$71 (RSI oversold) as Hormuz turned diplomatic — the war premium is gone. Gold eased and the dollar stayed firm; India's June CPI likely topped the RBI's 4% target and the ADB cut India's FY27 growth to 6.6%.

catalysts_today.event: Strait of Hormuz — any escalation would re-ignite the oil premium
SBI Funds Management IPO opens (to 16 July)
More India Q1 results — Infosys, HCLTech, Wipro and IT peers

favorites.name: Apple Inc.
favorites.whats_new: Essentially flat (-0.3% to $315), pausing just below its 52-week high after the recent run — it now sits at the 99th percentile of its one-year range, so momentum is strong but the stock is stretched and short-term overbought.

news.headline: US stocks hold at highs as the war premium fades and the VIX slips to ~15

favorites.fundamentals: Mega-cap (~$4.63T) at a rich P/E of 38 (forward 33) — investors are paying up for growth. Revenue growing ~17% a year with ~27% margins; beta ~1.1 means it moves roughly with the market. Dividend yield ~0.3%.
favorites.technicals: A confirmed uptrend (Strong Buy): price ($315) sits above its 50-day (~$298) and 200-day (~$272) averages, and all 10 moving averages are bullish. RSI 63 is neutral-to-strong, but Stochastic, CCI and Williams %R are all overbought — a short-term caution flag near the highs. First support ~$313, then the 50-day ~$298; first resistance ~$317.
favorites.catalysts: Its own earnings later in the season, the Apple-vs-OpenAI trade-secrets suit, and the broad tech tape.
favorites.risks: Overbought signals at a 52-week high raise the odds of a pullback; a rich valuation leaves little room for disappointment, and a renewed risk-off shock would hit high-fliers first.

favorites.name: NVIDIA Corp.
favorites.whats_new: Broke out — up 4% to $211 and, crucially, closed back above its 50-day average (~$209) for the first time in weeks, flipping the trend from 'stuck sideways/Neutral' to a confirmed uptrend (Strong Buy). SK Hynix's record $26.5bn AI-memory debut and easing risk appetite lit the fuse.

news.headline: SK Hynix soars ~13% in a record $26.5bn Nasdaq debut, reviving the AI-chip trade

favorites.fundamentals: Mega-cap (~$5.11T) at a P/E of 32 (forward 16, implying strong expected profit growth). Revenue growing ~85% a year with ~63% margins; beta 2.21 makes it far more volatile than the market. Tiny 0.5% dividend.
favorites.technicals: Uptrend regained: price ($211) is now above both its 50-day (~$209) and 200-day (~$192) averages, with all 10 moving averages bullish and MACD momentum positive. RSI 57 is neutral, but trend strength is weak (ADX 17), so expect choppy action, and Stochastic/Williams %R are overbought short-term. A close back below ~$209 would warn the breakout is failing; first resistance ~$214, first support ~$205.
favorites.catalysts: AI-chip demand read-through from the SK Hynix listing and peers, China H200 approval details, and Nvidia's next earnings.
favorites.risks: The breakout is fresh and trend strength is weak, so it could fail; high beta means sharp swings, 'AI fatigue' chatter is building, and a risk-off shock would hit it hard.

favorites.name: Bitcoin
favorites.whats_new: Barely moved (+0.4% to ~$64,393), holding near $64k despite continued spot-ETF outflows — but it's still below its 50- and 200-day averages, so this is a pause inside a downtrend, not a turn.

news.headline: Bitcoin holds near $64,000 despite ETF outflows; crypto market resilient

favorites.fundamentals: Roughly $1.29T in total value. No cash flows or earnings — its price is driven by risk appetite, interest-rate expectations and flows into/out of spot Bitcoin ETFs.
favorites.technicals: Downtrend intact: price (~$64,393) is below both its 50-day (~$65,146) and 200-day (~$73,988) averages and sits near the bottom of its 52-week range (11th percentile). Trend strength is strong (ADX 25) and pointing down, so down-moves have tended to persist; RSI 55 is neutral and MACD is improving. It needs a close above ~$65,146 to signal a real turn; first support ~$64,099.
favorites.catalysts: Spot-Bitcoin-ETF flows, the tone of the 29 July Fed decision (rate-cut hopes fuel crypto), and the broad risk-on/off swing.
favorites.risks: Still a confirmed downtrend near one-year lows; continued ETF outflows and a tight Fed would cap rebounds, and it trades like a high-beta risk asset, so it falls fast on any renewed shock.

favorites.name: Reliance Industries
favorites.whats_new: Bounced 2.2% to ₹1,308 with the broad India rally and the rating nudged up to Neutral (from Strong Sell), but it stays technically weak — still below all its major averages, near the bottom of its 52-week range. As an energy/refining major it's cross-exposed to the very oil swings driving the market.

news.headline: Sensex jumps 827 points as India rallies a second day; oil eases

favorites.fundamentals: Mega-cap (~₹17.7T) in energy, at a moderate P/E of 22 (forward 18). Revenue growing ~12% a year with thin ~8% margins (typical of refining); beta 0.18 makes it much steadier than the market. Dividend yield ~0.5%.
favorites.technicals: Downtrend, though improving: price (₹1,308) is below its 50-day (~₹1,332) and 200-day (~₹1,416) averages and sits in the 15th percentile of its 52-week range. RSI 49 is neutral and MACD momentum has turned positive. Today's pop lifted it above its 10- and 30-day averages; first resistance ~₹1,317, then the 50-day; first support ~₹1,293.
favorites.catalysts: Crude-price direction (refining margins), any energy/retail/Jio updates, and its own Q1 results later in the season.
favorites.risks: Weak technical structure near 52-week lows; the bounce needs to reclaim the 50-day (~₹1,332) to mean anything, and a fresh oil spike could squeeze refining economics.

risks: Hormuz re-escalation: the truce is verbal, not signed — any fresh incident (a mined tanker, a strike) would snap the oil 'war premium' back and reverse the rally.
Foreign selling: FIIs have pulled ~60% of their India inflows since 2024; if domestic SIP flows falter, the home-grown bid holding up the market could thin.
Sticky inflation: India's June CPI likely topped the RBI's 4% target, and higher-for-longer rates (a hawkish Warsh Fed, decision 29 July) keep pressure on rate-sensitive stocks and crypto.
AI-trade fatigue: SK Hynix and Nvidia rallied, but 'AI fatigue' chatter and stretched valuations mean the leaders can swing hard on any disappointment.
Crypto downtrend intact: Bitcoin is flat but still below key averages near one-year lows amid ETF outflows — the bounce can fade if risk appetite turns.
disclaimer: For information only. Not financial advice. Data may be delayed and can be inaccurate; verify before acting.
